summary: I, Maria Camila Palacio, was born in 1992 in Bogotá, Colombia. In 2015, I moved to Chicago to pursue my master's degree at Loyola University Chicago. Like my grandparents, I have my own traveling stories and have met people from all over the world, which has enriched my life greatly. I look forward to returning home with many stories to share with future generations of my family.

Bogotá is where I was born and where my educational journey continued after my grandfather Eduardo Palacio earned a scholarship to study at a university there. It is the capital of Colombia and holds a special place in my family's history. My connection to Bogotá is both personal and academic, as it shaped much of my family's story before I moved abroad.

Colombia is my home country, where I was born and raised in a big family full of stories. My grandparents' travels within and outside Colombia, like my grandfather Eduardo's journey from Ituango to Medellín, reflect the diverse experiences within the country. Colombia's culture and history are deeply woven into my identity and family narratives.

In 2015, I moved to Chicago to attend Loyola University Chicago for my master's degree. Living in Chicago has been transformative, teaching me independence and exposing me to an international community. This city became a new chapter in my life filled with diverse friendships and stories from around the world.

I enrolled at Loyola University Chicago in 2015 to pursue my master's degree. This institution became the setting for my growth as an independent adult and a place where I met many international friends. Loyola has played a significant role in shaping my recent life experiences in Chicago.

Héctor Abad Faciolince is a Colombian author whose words resonate with me deeply. He once wrote that in a big family, fiction is almost unnecessary because everything has happened at least once. His perspective perfectly captures the essence of my own family's rich and varied stories.

My grandfather Alfonso Chivivi was a remarkable traveler who journeyed around the world. One of his most fascinating experiences was living in Buenos Aires as a personal guest of President Juan Domingo Perón after attending a state dinner. His clever gift of emeralds to Evita Perón earned him six months living at the government's expense, a story that has always amazed me.

Buenos Aires is where my grandfather Alfonso Chivivi lived for six months as a personal guest of President Juan Domingo Perón. This happened after he attended a state dinner there representing Colombian jeweler unions and gifted emeralds to Evita Perón. His time in Buenos Aires was a unique chapter in our family's travel history.

Juan Domingo Perón was the president of Argentina when my grandfather Alfonso Chivivi became his personal guest. Alfonso had not known Perón before but was invited to a state dinner as a Colombian union representative. This connection led to Alfonso living in Argentina for six months, an incredible story in our family history.

Evita Perón, the wife of President Juan Domingo Perón, received a gift of emeralds from my grandfather Alfonso Chivivi during a state dinner in Buenos Aires. This gift helped my grandfather become a personal guest of the Perón family for six months. Her role in this story is a memorable part of my family's travel legacy.

My other grandfather, Eduardo Palacio, grew up in Ituango, a small town in Antioquia. At 14, he traveled by mule for four days to reach Medellín, where he was amazed by the city's modernity, especially cars. He earned scholarships to finish high school in Medellín and later to study at a university in Bogotá, where he met my grandmother.

Ituango is a small town in the mountains of Antioquia where my grandfather Eduardo Palacio was born and raised. When he was 14, he undertook a challenging four-day mule journey from Ituango to Medellín because there were no roads connecting the two places. Ituango represents the humble beginnings of my grandfather's inspiring journey.

The Antioquia Department is where my grandfather Eduardo Palacio grew up, specifically in the town of Ituango. It is a mountainous region in Colombia with Medellín as its capital, a city that dazzled my grandfather when he arrived there as a teenager. Antioquia's geography and culture are important parts of my family's roots.

Medellín is the capital of Antioquia and the city where my grandfather Eduardo Palacio arrived after a four-day mule trip from Ituango. He was amazed by the city's modernity, especially seeing cars for the first time. Medellín was where he earned scholarships to continue his education and begin a new chapter of his life.

While living in Chicago, I met friends from Georgia who shared their life stories and the history of their country with me. These friendships have enriched my experience abroad and expanded my understanding of different cultures. Georgia is part of the international family I have built here.

In Chicago, I have friends from Kosovo who have shared with me bits of their personal histories and the history of their country. These connections have broadened my perspective and made my time abroad more meaningful. Kosovo is one of the many places represented in my international circle.

Living in Chicago introduced me to friends from Laos who generously shared their life experiences and cultural background. These friendships have become part of my international family, enriching my understanding of the world. Laos represents one of the many diverse cultures I have encountered here.

Among my international friends in Chicago are people from Angola who have shared their stories and the history of their country with me. These relationships have added depth to my experience abroad and connected me to a wider global community. Angola is part of the diverse tapestry of cultures I have come to appreciate.

I have also made friends from China while living in Chicago, and they have shared with me their personal stories and cultural heritage. These friendships have enriched my life and broadened my worldview. China is among the many countries represented in my international family here.
